```json
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "A logistics system with strict carrier assignment rules is being asked to support automatic shipment splitting for heavy loads (>500kg), which directly conflicts with two foundational invariants: the one-carrier-per-shipment rule and the supervisor-only reassignment rule.",
    "bounded_context": "Shipment Management / Carrier Assignment",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Shipment", "definition": "A unit of cargo to be transported, currently guaranteed to have exactly one assigned Carrier." },
      { "term": "Carrier", "definition": "The entity responsible for transporting a Shipment. Assignment is 1:1 and locked post-assignment." },
      { "term": "Supervisor", "definition": "The only role authorized to reassign a Carrier on an existing Shipment." },
      { "term": "Sub-Shipment", "definition": "A proposed new concept — a child Shipment derived from splitting a parent Shipment. Does NOT exist in the current domain model." },
      { "term": "Auto-Split", "definition": "Proposed automated process to decompose a Shipment exceeding 500kg into multiple Sub-Shipments, each with a different Carrier, bypassing Supervisor authorization." },
      { "term": "Dispatch", "definition": "The point at which a Shipment is released for transport; the domain guarantees exactly one Carrier is assigned at this moment." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "A 501kg shipment contains a single indivisible item (e.g., one pallet, one engine block)", "expected_behavior": "Cannot be physically split — auto-split must fail or be prevented. The feature request does not address indivisible cargo." },
    { "scenario": "A shipment is split into sub-shipments and one sub-shipment's carrier fails delivery", "expected_behavior": "Unclear — who owns the original shipment's SLA? Partial delivery accountability is undefined." },
    { "scenario": "A shipment weighs exactly 500kg", "expected_behavior": "Ambiguous — is the threshold >500 or >=500? Off-by-one on weight threshold must be clarified." },
    { "scenario": "A shipment is split after carrier assignment but before dispatch", "expected_behavior": "Violates the locked-assignment invariant. The original carrier was assigned and locked, but the split effectively removes them from part of the cargo without supervisor approval." },
    { "scenario": "Customer queries tracking for a split shipment", "expected_behavior": "Undefined — customer expects one shipment, now has multiple. Tracking, invoicing, and proof-of-delivery are fragmented." },
    { "scenario": "Sub-shipments arrive at different times", "expected_behavior": "Undefined — does the original shipment's delivery date apply to each sub-shipment? What constitutes 'delivered'?" },
    { "scenario": "Regulatory or insurance implications of splitting", "expected_behavior": "Cargo insurance and customs declarations are per-shipment. Splitting may create compliance gaps." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Potential cost savings from utilizing multiple smaller/cheaper carriers for heavy shipments, but risk of increased operational overhead, split-delivery failures, and SLA penalty exposure from unclear accountability.",
    "operational": "Introduces a fundamentally new concept (parent-child shipment hierarchy) that ripples through tracking, invoicing, proof-of-delivery, returns, and dispute resolution. This is not a feature — it is a domain model evolution.",
    "user": "Customers who ship >500kg expect a single shipment with a single point of accountability. Auto-splitting without customer consent fragments their experience and may erode trust.",
    "risk": "HIGH. Two foundational invariants are violated simultaneously. The one-carrier-per-shipment guarantee and the supervisor-gated reassignment rule are load-bearing constraints — downstream systems (billing, tracking, SLA enforcement, insurance) depend on them. Bypassing both in an automated path creates systemic integrity risk.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Supervisors", "impact": "Their gatekeeper authority is bypassed for an entire class of shipments. This undermines the control model they rely on for accountability and risk management." },
      { "group": "Carriers", "impact": "A carrier may be auto-assigned to a sub-shipment they did not agree to. Carrier capacity, route compatibility, and contractual terms are not addressed." },
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "A single shipment silently becomes multiple deliveries. Tracking, invoicing, and delivery expectations are fragmented without consent." },
      { "group": "Operations / Dispatch", "impact": "Must now manage parent-child shipment relationships, partial deliveries, and multi-carrier coordination — a significant complexity increase." },
      { "group": "Finance / Billing", "impact": "Billing model assumes one shipment = one carrier = one invoice line. Split shipments break this assumption." },
      { "group": "Compliance / Legal", "impact": "Insurance policies and regulatory filings (customs, hazmat) are per-shipment. Splitting without review may create coverage gaps." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 40,
    "reasoning": "The feature request directly contradicts two established production invariants. This is not an ambiguity in interpretation — it is a specification-level contradiction. The request also introduces undefined concepts (sub-shipment, parent-child hierarchy) without addressing cascading impacts on billing, tracking, SLA, insurance, or customer experience. Confidence is low because shipping this as described would break the domain model.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "The one-carrier-per-shipment invariant is violated by auto-split",
      "The supervisor-only reassignment invariant is bypassed",
      "A parent-child shipment concept does not exist in the current model and would require domain model evolution"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Whether the product team intends sub-shipments to be full Shipment aggregates or a new lightweight entity",
      "How SLAs, billing, tracking, and insurance should work for split shipments",
      "Whether customer consent is required before splitting",
      "Whether the 500kg threshold is a hard physical constraint or a policy tunable"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "The existing invariants (one-carrier, supervisor-gated reassignment) are intentional and load-bearing, not accidental constraints",
      "The product team may not have fully considered the domain model implications of this request"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "contradiction",
      "description": "The auto-split feature requires assigning multiple carriers to what was a single shipment, directly violating the invariant that every Shipment has exactly one Carrier. This is not a relaxation of a policy — it breaks a structural guarantee that billing, tracking, SLA enforcement, and insurance depend on.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Operations", "Carriers", "Customers", "Finance", "Compliance"],
      "options": [
        "A) Reject auto-split as proposed. Heavy shipments require manual supervisor-mediated splitting.",
        "B) Introduce a new aggregate (e.g., ShipmentGroup or FreightOrder) that contains multiple Shipments. Each child Shipment still has exactly one Carrier, preserving the invariant. The parent entity owns the customer-facing identity, SLA, and billing.",
        "C) Relax the one-carrier invariant system-wide to allow multi-carrier shipments. This is a fundamental domain model change requiring impact analysis across all bounded contexts."
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B. Introduce a parent aggregate (e.g., FreightOrder) that owns the customer relationship, SLA, and billing. Auto-split creates child Shipments under it, each with exactly one Carrier. This preserves both invariants while enabling the business capability. Supervisor approval should still be required for the split decision — the 'auto' part can pre-compute the split plan, but a Supervisor confirms it."
    },
    {
      "type": "contradiction",
      "description": "The feature explicitly requests bypassing Supervisor involvement for carrier assignment changes. The existing model makes Supervisor approval a hard gate for any reassignment. Automating this away removes a critical accountability checkpoint.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Supervisors", "Operations", "Compliance"],
      "options": [
        "A) Keep supervisor approval mandatory, even for auto-split. The system proposes a split plan; the supervisor approves.",
        "B) Create a new authorization path: system-initiated splits for weight threshold are treated differently from manual reassignments.",
        "C) Remove the supervisor gate entirely for all reassignments (not recommended)."
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A. The system can auto-propose a split plan (carrier selection, weight distribution), but a Supervisor must approve before dispatch. This preserves the accountability model while reducing manual effort. If the business insists on fully automated flow, Option B is viable but requires explicit policy documentation of when supervisor oversight is and isn't required."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": [
    { "violation": "Auto-split assigns multiple carriers to a single shipment", "business_rule_broken": "A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier" },
    { "violation": "Auto-split bypasses supervisor authorization for carrier assignment changes", "business_rule_broken": "Only a Supervisor can reassign a Carrier on a Shipment" },
    { "violation": "Sub-Shipment concept does not exist in the domain model", "business_rule_broken": "No parent-child shipment hierarchy is defined — introducing it without domain model evolution creates undefined behavior in billing, tracking, SLA, and insurance" }
  ]
}
```
